title: RADIUS Authentication Latency SLO and Slow PSN Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(c) covers business continuity. PSN latency is operational continuity input.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: UC-17.1.40 alert log + monthly SLO report.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.9 (Protection and prevention) — robust ICT systems require demonstrable performance margins. SLO monitoring evidences this.
compliance.controlObjective: Operational performance margins for ICT system.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.5.30 ICT readiness for business continuity — performance monitoring evidences operational readiness.
A1.1 (Availability — capacity and demand) — latency-SLO evidence.

controlTest.positiveScenario: On a lab PSN, induce identity-store latency by inserting a network impairment (e.g. `tc qdisc add dev eth0 root netem delay 1500ms`) on the path between PSN and AD. The next batch of auths shows step2 latency spike; UC-17.1.40 fires within 15 min showing the PSN with `p95_lat > 2000`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Remove the impairment. Latency returns to baseline within 15 min. Alert clears on next schedule.

dataSources: ISE syslog `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` *Authentication* category. Every 5400 (auth success) and 5411/5417/5440 (auth failure) event carries the `Step Latency` extension fields recording per-step processing time inside ISE: `Step1Latency` (RADIUS receive→initial parse), `Step2Latency` (parsing→identity-store query), `Step3Latency` (identity-store query→policy evaluation), `Step4Latency` (policy eval→Access-Accept emit). Total is `TotalLatency`. Network Access Devices (NADs) also emit RADIUS server-response-time when configured: `index=network sourcetype=cisco:ios` codes `%RADIUS-3-NORESPONSE` and `%RADIUS-4-RADIUSDEAD`. CIM mapping: `Authentication.duration` after a calculated field aliases `TotalLatency` (in ms).
app: `Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services` ([Splunkbase 1915](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1915)) + `Splunk Add-on for Cisco IOS-XE/NX-OS`.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (5400, 5411, 5417)
| rex "NodeName=(?<psn>[^,\s]+)"
| rex "TotalLatency=(?<latency_ms>\d+)"
| rex "Step1Latency=(?<step1>\d+)"
| rex "Step2Latency=(?<step2>\d+)"
| rex "Step3Latency=(?<step3>\d+)"
| rex "Step4Latency=(?<step4>\d+)"
| eval latency_ms = tonumber(latency_ms)
| stats avg(latency_ms) as avg_lat perc95(latency_ms) as p95_lat perc99(latency_ms) as p99_lat avg(step1) as avg_step1 avg(step2) as avg_step2 avg(step3) as avg_step3 avg(step4) as avg_step4 count as auth_count by psn
| eval slo_breached = if(p95_lat > 2000, "yes", "no")
| where slo_breached=="yes" OR p99_lat > 5000
| sort - p95_lat
description: Detects ISE PSNs whose 95th-percentile total RADIUS authentication latency exceeds the SLO of 2000ms (or 99th-percentile exceeds 5000ms) over a rolling 15-minute window. Decomposes the latency into the 4 ISE processing steps so the bottleneck is identifiable: Step2 spike → identity-store slowness; Step3 spike → complex authorization policy.
value: When RADIUS latency exceeds NAD timeout (typically 5s), the NAD considers ISE 'dead' and either falls through to a backup ISE PSN, falls through to a local fallback (allow-all), or denies all auths. The downstream user impact: failed wireless auth, slow Wi-Fi roaming, dropped 802.1X sessions on switching. Catching the latency drift at p95>2s gives the platform team time to investigate before the timeouts start.
implementation: Confirm the *Authentication* category is forwarded at INFO. Most deployments already accept this data. Schedule the search every 5 min, search window `-15m@m to @m`, trigger on count > 0, throttle suppression on `psn` for 30 min.
visualization: (1) Per-PSN p95 latency timechart, last 24h, span=5m. SLO line at 2000ms. (2) Per-PSN p99 latency timechart, last 24h, span=5m. SLO line at 5000ms. (3) Stacked bar: average step latency breakdown per PSN — reveals which step is the bottleneck. (4) Top-N table: PSNs by p95 last 7d.
dataModelAcceleration: Yes — Authentication DM acceleration speeds the percentile calculations on >24h windows.

references.title: Cisco ISE 3.4 Admin Guide — Performance Monitoring
Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services (Splunkbase 1915)

knownFalsePositives: (a) **First 5 minutes after PSN restart** — JIT compilation and cache priming spike latency. Suppress per `psn` for 15 min after a 60030 event. (b) **Identity store provider degradation** — When AD or LDAP is slow, every PSN authenticating against it shows elevated step2 latency. Cross-reference UC-17.1.44 (identity store health) and treat ISE PSN as a victim, not a cause. (c) **Bulk reauth event** — A CRL push or SGT mapping change triggers reauth from many endpoints simultaneously, briefly saturating PSN auth queues. Cross-ref UC-17.1.18. (d) **Initial baseline after deployment scale-up** — Adding endpoints to an undersized PSN; latency rises until auth load redistributes after NAD RADIUS server-list reorder.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• *Authentication* category at INFO.
• `psn_inventory.csv` lookup with `psn`, `site`, `expected_p95_ms`.
• Authentication DM accelerated.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Validate latency fields are extracted:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode=5400 earliest=-1h
| eval has_lat = if(isnotnull(TotalLatency), "yes", "no")
| stats count by has_lat
```
If `has_lat=no` dominates, the TA's regex isn't extracting `TotalLatency`. Upgrade Splunkbase 1915.

Step 2 — Create the search
Primary search in `spl` field. Schedule every 5 min.

Step 3 — Validate
Provoke as above. Confirm step-latency decomposition shows step2 spike when AD is slow.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — RADIUS Latency SLO":
• Per-PSN p95 timechart with SLO line.
• Per-PSN p99 timechart with SLO line.
• Stacked bar: avg step latency per PSN.
• Top-N PSNs by p95.

Alerting:
• PagerDuty medium on individual PSN p95 > 2000ms sustained 15 min.
• PagerDuty high on fleet-wide p95 > 2000ms (all PSNs simultaneously — implies upstream identity-store issue).

Runbook:
1. Identify offending PSN.
2. Inspect step decomposition. If step2 dominates → identity-store issue (UC-17.1.44).
3. If step3 dominates → policy complexity. Review the matched authorization rules; consider ordering optimisation.
4. If step1/step4 dominate → ISE-internal queue saturation; correlate with UC-17.1.29 (resource saturation).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **TotalLatency missing** — Old TA version. Upgrade.
• **Latency reports look reasonable but NADs are timing out** — NAD's RADIUS timeout is set lower than 2000ms (e.g. 1500ms). Either tighten ISE SLO or relax NAD timeout. Consult NAD config: `radius-server timeout 5`.
• **One PSN consistently slow** — under-sized PSN handling more sessions than its capacity tier. Redistribute load via NAD RADIUS server-list reorder.
• **All PSNs spike at the same time** — Identity store (AD/LDAP) issue. Engage AD team.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how long the access-control system takes to say 'yes, you're allowed in' — like checking how fast a shop checkout works. If a checkout starts taking too long for too many customers, we ring the bell so a manager can investigate before the queue blocks the shop.
